# Managing Packages with renv

`renv` manages R package versions at the project level. Without it, R installs packages into a single system-wide library shared across all your projects. This means updating a package for one project can silently break another. `renv` solves this by giving each project its own isolated package library and recording the exact versions in a lockfile.

For CWER projects, this matters in two ways. First, it ensures that analyses are reproducible — anyone who clones a project and runs `renv::restore()` gets identical package versions. Second, it protects against analyses breaking when packages update, which is especially important for packages like `metafor`, `MplusAutomation`, and `flextable` where version differences can change results or output formatting.

## Setting Up renv for a New Project

With your project open in Positron, run in the R console:
```r
install.packages("renv")
renv::init()
```

`renv` scans your project for packages used in your scripts, installs them into a project-specific library, and creates two files:

- `renv.lock` — records the exact version of every package and its dependencies
- `.Rprofile` — automatically activates renv when R starts in this project

When renv is active, you will see this message at the start of every R session:
```
- Project '~/Documents/GitHub/my-project' loaded. [renv 1.2.0]
```

## Installing New Packages

Install packages as you normally would:
```r
install.packages("lme4")
```

Then snapshot to record the new package in the lockfile:
```r
renv::snapshot()
```

Commit `renv.lock` to GitHub after snapshotting so teammates get the updated package list.

## Restoring Packages

When a teammate clones the project and opens it for the first time, they restore all packages with:
```r
renv::restore()
```

This installs exactly the versions recorded in `renv.lock` — no more, no less.

## Checking Status

To see whether your project library is in sync with the lockfile:
```r
renv::status()
```

If packages have been installed but not snapshotted, or if the lockfile references packages not in the library, `renv::status()` will tell you. Run `renv::snapshot()` or `renv::restore()` to resolve the discrepancy.

## Updating Packages

To update a package to a newer version:
```r
renv::update("lme4")
renv::snapshot()
```

Always test your analysis after updating packages to make sure nothing broke. If something does break, you can revert to the previous lockfile state using Git.

## What Gets Committed to GitHub

| File / Folder | Commit? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `renv.lock` | Yes | Records package versions — essential for reproducibility |
| `.Rprofile` | Yes | Activates renv automatically |
| `renv/` | No | The package library itself — large and can be restored from lockfile |

Make sure `renv/library/` is in your `.gitignore`:
```
renv/library/
```

## Common Issues

**renv not activating.** If you do not see the renv activation message when R starts, check that `.Rprofile` exists in your project root and that you opened the project folder in Positron rather than just opening the script file directly.

**Package not found after restore.** Some packages require system dependencies (compiled libraries) that renv cannot install. If `renv::restore()` fails for a specific package, check the package documentation for system requirements and contact Mike or Joanna if you are unsure.

**Lockfile conflicts.** If two team members update `renv.lock` at the same time and push to GitHub, you may get a merge conflict on the lockfile. Resolve it by accepting one version and running `renv::restore()` to sync your library to the resolved lockfile.
